1:
The cloud of witnesses on John 2:1 converges on two related questions: what "the third day" counts from, and why the wedding setting matters theologically. The grammatical-historical commentators are most active on the chronological puzzle. Ellicott reckons the third day from the call of Philip in John 1:43, giving one clear intervening day; Robertson works out that seven days have elapsed since John 1:19 and places the wedding within that running sequence. Barnes, meanwhile, locates Cana about fifteen miles north-west of Tiberias and supplies a careful geographical note. John Lightfoot adds a distinctive Jewish legal backdrop: rabbinic convention required virgins to marry on a Wednesday, the fourth day of the week, so that a potential complaint about the bride's virginity could be brought before the Sanhedrin on Thursday — a detail that grounds the scene in the texture of first-century Palestinian custom.

On the theological significance of Christ's presence at a wedding, the Reformed and Wesleyan voices unite in seeing an implicit sanctification of ordinary human festivity. Calvin notes that this first miracle, already chosen with care by the Evangelist, repays close attention precisely because it inaugurates Jesus's public signs. Wesley draws a sharp practical point: Christ's attendance at the marriage feast shows that he does not abolish human society but consecrates it. Matthew Henry concurs, observing that the disciples needed "something wherewith to answer those that asked, What sign showest thou?" before many could be persuaded — so the Cana miracle meets a structural necessity in the Gospel's plan of disclosure.

Augustine's contribution stands somewhat apart and anticipates the whole sign's deeper register: the one who turned water to wine at Cana is the same who produces wine in the vine every year — nature and miracle are continuous acts of the same creative hand. This sacramental-theological note, characteristic of the Latin-Alexandrian tradition, lifts the episode beyond biography into a meditation on divine providence operating through natural and supernatural means alike. Chrysostom, for his part, registers the social modesty with which Jesus arrived at the feast — invited "not as a great person, but merely as one they knew" — and sees in this lowliness a deliberate divine condescension before the hour of glorification.
2:
On John 2:2 the witnesses are fewer in number but notably specific in their emphases. The grammatical-historical commentators anchor the verse carefully. Robertson notes that Jesus was invited ("was bidden," first aorist passive of καλέω) because his mother was already present, and plausibly suggests the invitation to the disciples followed naturally from their association with the family through Nathanael. Barnes correctly identifies the disciples named so far — Peter, Andrew, Philip, and Nathanael — and distinguishes their standing at this point: they are believers in the Messiah but not yet formally called to the apostolate. The miracle wrought here was designed, Barnes argues, to confirm rather than inaugurate their faith, deepening what their initial encounter had begun.

Adam Clarke seizes on the occasion to enumerate three features he deems historically significant. He describes this as "probably the first Christian wedding that was ever in the world" — the first nuptial occasion honoured by the physical presence of Jesus and his gathered followers. He counts the disciples at roughly four or five, given the sequence in John 1:37 and following. And he observes that the first miracle Jesus performed was done not in a synagogue or before religious authorities but at a domestic, social celebration — a detail that speaks to the breadth of Christ's concern for the whole of human life. Wesley draws the same practical conclusion: Christ's attendance at the marriage feast shows that he does not abolish human society but consecrates it, and that wine at a festival is not forbidden; the rougher disciplines of discipleship would come later, once these men had been drawn gently by grace.

Ellicott adds a perceptive social note: Eastern hospitality would naturally extend to those in Mary's company without requiring a separate formal invitation. The disciples needed no distinct summons to be welcomed into a household where their teacher's family was already known. Taken together, the witnesses frame this verse as a transitional threshold moment — the first small gathering of what would become the church, sitting at table with their Lord, on the eve of the sign that would strengthen their nascent faith.
3:
The voices on John 2:3 concentrate on two questions: why the wine ran out, and what Mary intended by reporting the shortage to Jesus. On the first, John Gill traces the deficiency to the poverty of the family, the unexpected arrival of extra guests, and — crucially — to divine providence arranging the occasion so that a miracle would be possible. Barnes notes that a Jewish wedding feast could last seven or eight days, so the wine shortage may have occurred not at the feast's outset but well into the celebration, explaining why Jesus might have arrived partway through. Robertson identifies the Greek participle for the wine's failure (ὑστερήσαντος, genitive absolute) as a precise technical note that something expected has fallen short — a word he connects with its use in Mark 10:21, where "one thing lacking" marks the rich young man's deficiency.

The debate over Mary's motive is sharper. Calvin is the most cautious: he doubts she was expecting a miracle — since Christ had performed none as yet — and proposes she may simply have been suggesting that Jesus offer some pious exhortations to ease the guests' embarrassment rather than expecting a supernatural remedy. Yet he does not press this reading with full confidence. Clarke reads Mary as already intuiting her son's power and goodness from long acquaintance, expecting "something extraordinary" without perhaps knowing its precise form. Ellicott frames her statement as fixing its own meaning by what follows: the next verses reveal that her remark carried an expectation of supernatural intervention, consistent with what a mother who had pondered the angel's words across thirty years would naturally lean toward. Wesley holds the possibilities explicitly open — either "supply them by miracle" or "go away so others may also leave" — without forcing a resolution.

The broader Reformed and puritan-evangelical reading finds the providential element decisive: the shortage is not a mishap but an occasion arranged by providence so that human need would become the occasion of the first sign. Mary's words, whatever she consciously intended, function in the narrative economy as the human petition through which divine grace finds its entry point. The shortage creates the space the miracle requires; in this sense the poverty of the hosts is itself part of the Evangelist's theology of abundance.
4:
John 2:4 — Jesus's address to his mother, "Woman, what have I to do with thee? Mine hour is not yet come" — is the most contested verse in the pericope, and Ellicott correctly notes it as "an old battle-ground between Protestant and Romanist expositors." The grammatical-historical commentators lead with linguistic precision. Robertson explains that γύναι (vocative of γυνή) carries no censure: Jesus uses the same form tenderly at John 19:26. By addressing Mary as "Woman" rather than "Mother," Robertson argues, Jesus signals that she can no longer exercise maternal authority over his Messianic vocation — a lesson that is "difficult for mothers and fathers to learn, when to let go." Barnes concurs that no disrespect is implied: the same address appears at John 20:15 in a moment of profound tenderness.

The Reformed school reads the rebuke as a necessary theological correction even while acknowledging Mary's virtue. Calvin's analysis is careful: her concern for the guests was humane and praiseworthy, but by pressing her Son in his Messianic office she overstepped her bounds — she failed to consider that Christ acts not as a private individual but as the Father's appointed agent. Wesley, from the Wesleyan-Arminian side, takes a similar but slightly softer line: "a mild reproof of her inordinate concern and untimely interposal," noting that Jesus consistently held his Father's authority above family claims. Adam Clarke reads the phrase τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί (what is this to thee and me?) as an abrupt but proper reminder that they had not been employed to supply this feast — the provision was someone else's responsibility.

John Chrysostom's contribution is curiously oblique in the condensed source but pivots to the freedom of Christ from external compulsion: the fact that he subsequently grants the miracle before "the hour" he declared unready actually demonstrates he is not under subjection to any fixed external timetable — the hour belongs to his own sovereign will. The JFB entry at this verse appears to treat the prodigal son rather than Cana directly, which is an outlier voice misdirected in its source; the genuine exegetical weight here rests with Robertson, Calvin, Barnes, and Wesley on the question of what "Woman" signals about the Messianic independence of Christ from maternal authority.
5:
On John 2:5 — "Whatsoever he saith unto you, do it" — the witnesses are broadly united in reading Mary's command to the servants as evidence that Jesus's response in v.4, though apparently a refusal, was received by her as a veiled signal of intent. Gill judges that she "took the reproof in good part," inferring from his words, manner, or look that the request would be met, and so acted immediately. Clarke traces the same logic: though it was not yet time for the miracle (the want had not yet become general), Mary received the implicit caution and quietly anticipated the divine wisdom's moment. Ellicott reinforces this, noting that her command to the servants confirms she was connected to the household — an authority over the staff she would not naturally possess as a mere guest.

The Reformed tradition attends to the verse's broader theological import. Calvin sees Mary's deference to her Son as a paradigm case of true filial obedience — not now the obedience of a mother to a child in domestic matters, but the obedience of a creature to her Lord in matters of divine power. He notes that she "modestly acquiesces" in the reply and then "exhorts others to comply with his injunctions" — turning from petitioner to evangelist in the same breath. Barnes draws the same inference about the relation between v.4 and v.5: Jesus's words were not a harsh repulse but an indication of willingness at the proper time, and the transaction as a whole shows "the appropriate feelings of a son toward a mother."

Robertson captures the Greek syntax precisely: the command is framed as an indefinite relative sentence with the aorist imperative of ποιέω — instant, complete obedience at whatever moment he should speak. Chrysostom circles back to the theological point set up in his comment on v.4: that Christ afterwards did what his mother told him "in order to show plainly, that He was not under subjection to the hour" — his compliance was not capitulation to her pressure but sovereign grace honoring a parent before showing himself Lord over nature. Wesley, typically concise, reads her statement as evidence that she had gathered from his answer that something extraordinary was about to happen.
6:
On John 2:6 the witnesses are largely agreed that the six stone water-pots for Jewish purification rites supply both the raw material of the miracle and an implicit theological contrast between legal purification and Messianic abundance. The grammatical-historical commentators attend carefully to physical detail. Robertson identifies the jars (ὑδρίαι) as stone vessels known also from papyri and connects their use to the Markan purification scenes of Mark 7:3 and the washing customs described in Luke 11:39. Barnes supplies the Jewish background: these jars served the ceremonial handwashing required before and after eating and the ritual washing of vessels as codified in the Law. That there were six of them he takes as a natural detail of a large and well-attended feast rather than any symbolically loaded number.

Lightfoot supplies the most specialized commentary: from the Talmudic tractates he demonstrates that stone vessels were considered capable of contracting impurity yet were also particularly suited to purification — a halakhic detail that explains why stone, rather than pottery, is specified by the Evangelist. He also judges the number six to reflect the scale of attendance rather than any fixed national custom for wedding celebrations. Clarke presses the Greek preposition κατά in the phrase "after the manner of the purifying of the Jews" toward a final-cause reading — not merely "in the manner of" but "for the purpose of" — giving the phrase the sense that these jars existed expressly to meet Jewish purity obligations. Ellicott notes that they were placed in the outer court rather than the guest-chamber, which explains why the governor of the feast was unaware of what had occurred.

Calvin focuses on the quantity — computing from the metrēta and comparing both the Attic and Hebrew measures — to establish that the total wine produced was very considerable indeed, a volume that magnifies the miracle's scope beyond any domestic need. Wesley confines himself to the single explanatory note that the Jews "purified themselves by frequent washings particularly before eating." Together the witnesses use the stone jars to frame the miracle within the whole apparatus of legal purification: what the law prescribed for ritual cleansing, Christ replaces with the wine of Messianic joy — the water of obligation becoming the wine of grace.
7:
The witnesses on John 2:7 — Jesus's command "Fill the waterpots with water … up to the brim" — concentrate on two features: the deliberate transparency of the miracle's method, and what the completeness of the filling signifies. The grammatical-historical commentators are most precise on the mechanics. Robertson parses the verb γεμίσατε as an effective first-aorist imperative of γεμίζω, indicating complete, thoroughgoing filling, and observes that the phrase "up to the brim" (ἕως ἄνω) is the direct counterpart of "down to the bottom" in Matthew 27:51 — no room left at all. Barnes makes the apologetic point explicitly: the servants who filled the jars were not disciples of Jesus, so there was "no opportunity of saying that the disciples of Jesus had filled them with wine to produce the appearance of a miracle" — the transparent method removes the most obvious objection.

Calvin attends to the pedagogical dimension of the command: Christ often gives instructions that seem initially pointless or even absurd, so that his power may be "more illustriously displayed by an unexpected result." The servants already had more than enough water; the instruction to add more could have seemed redundant. But this is precisely the pattern of the divine economy — requiring human participation up to its fullest extent before the transformation is manifest. The completeness of the filling, on Calvin's reading, also magnifies the miracle itself: the vessels that were drawn from, having just been filled to the brim with water, could leave no doubt in anyone's mind about the nature of what they previously contained.

Lightfoot raises an important contextual note about the exchange between Jesus and Mary in v.4: he judges it probable that this conversation was private, not public before the whole company, which would make Jesus's address "Woman, what have I to do with thee?" less harsh in context. Ellicott observes that the jars were "wholly or in part empty," the water having been used for the ablutions before the feast — reinforcing why filling to the brim carried particular significance for those watching. The command here is thus both simple and profound: a deliberate, witnessed, unambiguous replacement of the empty purification water with something altogether new.
8:
The witnesses on John 2:8 — "Draw out now, and bear unto the governor of the feast" — concentrate on the significance of choosing the ruler of the feast as the first taster, and on what the servants' obedient swiftness reveals. Barnes captures the core apologetic logic: by directing the servants to carry the drawn wine immediately to the ruler — without parade, without approaching the water-pots himself, apparently willing the change at a distance — Jesus demonstrates that the miracle is "a clear exhibition of divine power." He "willed it, and it was done." The governor of the feast, knowing nothing of what had occurred, is therefore the ideal impartial witness: his judgment cannot be biased by prior knowledge or social deference to the miracle-worker.

Adam Clarke offers a careful historical note on the ἀρχιτρίκλινος, the ruler of the feast: the title derives from "head over three couches or tables," reflecting the Oriental custom of reclining on low couches rather than sitting at raised tables, with groups of three arranged together. The governor's role was to oversee the ordering of the feast — distributing food and drink, managing the flow of the banquet. Ellicott adds that the actual mechanism was to let a smaller vessel down into the large jar and carry that to the ruler, who would distribute from it to the guests.

Calvin draws a purposive observation from the choice of destination: Christ "wished that the flavor of the wine should be tried by [the governor] before it had been tasted by himself, or by any other of the guests." This routing through the feast's impartial authority, combined with the ready obedience of the servants who had watched the whole process, becomes for Calvin a double authentication: the servants' alacrity shows the "great reverence and respect in which he was held by them," and the governor's ignorance makes his coming verdict unimpeachable. Robertson focuses on the Greek verb ἀντλέω (to draw) and the detail that the servants seem instructed to draw from the large water-jars now full, making the chain of custody from water to wine through known vessels a single unbroken line.
9:
On John 2:9 the witnesses are thin in number but press a single point with notable unanimity: the ruler of the feast's ignorance of the miracle's source is precisely what makes his testimony to the wine's quality decisive and unimpeachable. Barnes states the interpretive logic plainly — the tablemaster's judgment was "not biased by any favour, or any want of favour, toward Jesus." Had he known what had been done, questions of social deference or prior sympathy might cloud his verdict; because he did not know, his testimony stands as that of a competent expert uncontaminated by prior knowledge, and it pronounces the wine of the finest quality the feast had seen.

Ellicott presses a more delicate exegetical question: at exactly what moment did the transformation from water to wine take place? The narrator speaks of "water that had become wine" (τὸ ὕδωρ οἶνον γεγενημένον) at the point of the ruler's tasting, yet in the parenthetical note about the servants immediately refers back to it as "water." Ellicott reads this as authentic eyewitness restraint — the narrator deliberately refrains from specifying the moment of change because no one actually observed it happen; the servants knew they had drawn from vessels they had filled with water, and the ruler knew only that he was tasting excellent wine. The gap between what the servants saw and what the ruler tasted is the silence in which the miracle occurred. Robertson supplies the Greek grammar: γεγενημένον is a perfect passive participle of γίνομαι in a predicative accusative construction, indicating a completed, stable transformation — the water had fully and irreversibly become wine.

Gill's small contribution is nonetheless precise: the Persian version simply reads "he tasted of the wine," collapsing the narrator's careful dual notation, whereas the Greek text preserves the charged phrase "water that had become wine" — marking simultaneously what it was before and what it now permanently is. Wesley identifies the governor's social function: the friend appointed by the bridegroom to manage the entertainment, whose professional competence in such matters makes his judgment the natural instrument of public attestation for the sign Jesus had wrought.
10:
The testimony on John 2:10 — the ruler's remark that hosts customarily serve the best wine first, keeping the inferior for when guests have "well drunk" — generates the chapter's sharpest lexical debate: does μεθυσθῶσιν imply mere satiety or actual intoxication? The grammatical-historical commentators engage this directly. Ellicott marshals the evidence from the LXX usage of the same verb in Genesis 43:34, where the Authorised Version renders it "were merry," and from its New Testament occurrences in Matthew 24:49, Acts 2:15, 1 Corinthians 11:21, Ephesians 5:18, 1 Thessalonians 5:7, and Revelation 17:6, concluding that the verb in its New Testament usage uniformly implies drunkenness rather than mere merriness. Barnes takes a more irenic position: the word "does not of necessity mean that they were intoxicated," but may indicate drinking sufficient to diminish the keenness of taste — so that "hilarity" rather than inebriation is all that needs to be inferred for the observation to hold.

The practical point at stake is whether the miracle implies divine approval of drinking to excess. Wesley defuses this by limiting the phrase: "well drunk" means no more than "toward the close of the entertainment" — a temporal rather than a qualitative judgment, indicating that the good wine arrived late, not that the guests were in any objectionable state. He adds a careful narratorial note: "St. John barely relates the words [the governor] spoke, which does not imply his approving them" — the Evangelist records the custom without endorsing the practice it describes. Adam Clarke focuses instead on the quality of Christ's wine: perfectly pure and highly nutritive, which for Clarke may carry a hint that its excellence would have been apparent at any stage of the feast.

Gill confines himself to noting that the governor's words describe "the common custom used at feasts," and JFB contributes only the briefest phrase about wine being "kept for festive occasions." The witnesses are agreed that the ruler's remark is being deployed by John as testimony to the superior quality of what Christ produced — the debate is only over what the host's generalisation about the feast's guests actually implies.
11:
On John 2:11 the witnesses converge with unusual unanimity on two claims that the Evangelist himself signals: this was the first of Jesus's "signs," and its effect was to strengthen the faith of the disciples who were present. Calvin establishes that the term "beginning of miracles" (ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων) means the absolutely first sign in Christ's public ministry — the infancy narratives contain no miracles wrought by Christ himself, properly speaking, since those performed by angels and stars belonged to attendant divine action rather than to the Son's own agency. Robertson reinforces this grammatically, noting that the Greek construction (ταύτην ἐποίησεν ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων) — without an article between ταύτην and ἀρχήν — reads "this Jesus did as a beginning of his signs," marking it as the first in a sequence that John carefully tracks through the Gospel.

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices alike attend to John's characteristic word choice. Where the Synoptics use "wonders" (τέρατα), John reserves the term σημεῖα ("signs") — events that point beyond themselves to the identity and mission of the sign-worker. Barnes spells out what was signified: "his power, and proper character as the Messiah." Clarke specifies still more precisely: the sign manifested his "supreme Divinity," connecting back to the Prologue's confession at John 1:14 that the Word became flesh and the disciples "beheld his glory." The wedding miracle is thus read as the first visible confirmation of that glory.

The effect on the disciples is registered across the witnesses. Wesley's spare comment — "his disciples believed more steadfastly" — captures a consensus that all the more detailed commentators share. John Gill notes that this is "the first miracle Christ ever wrought, either in public or private," dismissing the infancy miracles of apocryphal tradition as unworthy of credit. Matthew Henry, while commenting on the broader pericope, sets the sign within the economy of disclosure: without miracles, few would have been persuaded that Jesus was the Messiah. The Cana sign, on this reading, is not merely a domestic kindness but the inaugural act of a deliberate campaign of self-revelation through which the disciples — and through John's Gospel the reader — are led to faith.
12:
On John 2:12 — the brief notice of Jesus's descent to Capernaum with his mother, brothers, and disciples — the most contested question among the witnesses is the identity of the "brothers" (ἀδελφοί). The grammatical-historical commentators address this carefully but without uniform resolution. Robertson notes that the brothers appear "friendly at first," suggesting he is aware of the later strains recorded in John 7:5, but does not resolve the cousins-vs.-siblings question here. Calvin, representing the Reformed position, implies natural blood brothers and moves quickly to the stay's brevity. Ellicott observes that the phrase "after this" allows for a return to Nazareth between Cana and Capernaum, which would account for Mary and the brothers joining the party.

The patristic voices introduce a distinct emphasis on purpose and movement. Chrysostom interprets the brief Capernaum visit as a deliberate decision by Christ not to take his mother and brothers everywhere about with him — a sign that family relationships would not structure his itinerant ministry. Alcuin, whose comment at this verse is really concerned with the following temple episode, uses the journey to Jerusalem as an example of "careful obedience to the Divine commands," noting that the Son of God fulfilled the law he had given. Augustine, whose surviving comment here has migrated from another context, reflects on the paradox of the Lord who is both high (as creator) and low (as servant) — a christological meditation prompted by movement between these scenes.

Origen raises the pointed lexical question that will recur at v.13: why does John write "the Passover of the Jews"? Of what other nation was it a feast? Origen finds in the qualifier a signal that this Passover, properly belonging to the whole people of God, has in some sense been evacuated of its deepest meaning by the very community charged with guarding it — and that its true fulfilment awaits the one who is now descending into the landscape of its celebration. Matthew Henry notes practically that Capernaum was "about a day's journey from Cana," identifying it as the base of operations Jesus would use throughout his Galilean ministry.
13:
On John 2:13 — "And the Jews' Passover was at hand, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem" — the witnesses focus on two issues: the chronological significance of this Passover in Jesus's ministry, and the question of whether the temple cleansing that follows is the same event as the one recorded in the Synoptics at the end of Jesus's ministry. On the chronological question, Adam Clarke provides the most precise counting: this is the first Passover after Christ's baptism; the second is mentioned in Luke 6:1; the third at John 6:4; and the fourth — the Passover of his crucifixion — is that narrated at the passion. Robertson observes that John, writing after the destruction of the temple for Gentile readers, uses "the Passover of the Jews" where the Synoptics would simply say "the Passover" — the qualifier signals an audience that needs the festival explained.

Origen's theological meditation on the phrase "of the Jews" runs deeper: he asks what other nation's Passover this could possibly be, and finds in the qualifier a subtle indictment — the feast that belonged by divine appointment to the whole people of God has, by the time John writes, become a badge of one ethnic community's religious identity, a community that is about to reject the fulfilment toward which the feast had always pointed. This reading, characteristic of the eastern-Alexandrian school, interprets the Passover typologically: John writes "of the Jews" precisely because the Passover is about to find its true meaning in the one who now ascends to Jerusalem.

On the question of one or two cleansings, the witnesses divide. Barnes affirms two separate events at the beginning and end of Jesus's ministry; Calvin likewise sees the difference of time as demonstrating the two accounts describe distinct occurrences. Adam Clarke is more hesitant, suggesting John may be anticipating three years of time in a single narrative placement. Ellicott directs the reader to his notes on Matthew's account and the chronological introduction without resolving the question definitively, though his general inclination favours two separate cleansings. The Reformed and grammatical-historical commentators thus share a default assumption of two events, while Clarke raises the question without fully settling it.
14:
The witnesses on John 2:14 are largely agreed on the commercial and liturgical context of the temple scene, while diverging on whether this is the same event as the Synoptic cleansing. Gill provides the most careful spatial analysis: the traders were not in the holy place, the court of priests, or the court of women, but in the court of the Gentiles, the outermost area within the temple precinct (τὸ ἱερόν, not the ναός proper). Robertson confirms this identification and lists the traders by category — those selling oxen (βόας), sheep (πρόβατα), and doves (περιστεράς), together with the money-changers (κερματιστάς) who converted foreign currency into the Tyrian shekel required for the annual temple tax. The outer court had in effect become a livestock market and bureau de change serving the practical needs of pilgrims arriving from distant regions who could not transport sacrificial animals on a long journey.

Bede opens the verse with a devotional application that none of the other commentators pursue: Jesus entered the temple immediately upon arriving in Jerusalem, offering believers the example that wherever they travel, the house of God should receive the first visit. He further observes that the animals sold in the precincts were prescribed by Mosaic law for sacrifice — a divinely condescending provision for the people's weakness — but what had begun as a regulated convenience had degenerated into commercial exploitation of the very space set apart for prayer and the drawing near of the nations. Wesley identifies the categories of commerce with functional brevity: animals for sacrifice, money-changers meeting the exchange requirement imposed by the temple's monetary system.

Chrysostom uses the encounter to look ahead to the "My Father's house" declaration of v.16 and the resurrection claim of v.19: Jesus's language of divine sonship is tolerated here because the crowd reads it in a general and conventional sense, whereas later, more explicit assertions of equality with God will provoke their murderous rage. The temple cleansing is therefore the opening move in a sustained Messianic self-disclosure — conducted through the language of sonship and sacred space — whose full significance would only be unlocked for the disciples when the tomb stood empty on the third day.
15:
The voices on John 2:15 are few but they address a genuinely contested question: was the scourge of cords used against people or only against the animals? Ellicott supplies the key textual correction: the verse should read "both the sheep and the oxen" as the objects driven out with the physical whip, not "all — men and sheep and oxen." The change of one Greek word (τε καί rather than simply καί) gives an entirely different sense, making it clear that the animals received the coercion of the scourge while the human traders were expelled by the force of commanding moral authority alone. Robertson confirms this parsing and the significance of τε καί, noting that the construction applies the scourge explicitly to both sheep and oxen together, not to all persons in the court.

Barnes reads the scourge as "an emblem of authority" at least as much as a practical instrument for moving livestock, and is explicit that "there is no evidence that he used any violence to the men engaged in that unhallowed traffic." The authority with which Jesus spoke and acted was sufficient to move the traders themselves without physical compulsion — a point that all the commentators present at this verse implicitly share. The moral force of the act was its most remarkable feature, exceeding any physical dimension. Gill raises the practical question of where the cords came from — possibly from the hides of slaughtered sacrifice animals, or from the ropes with which owners had led and tethered their livestock into the court. The whip is plaited on the spot from materials the temple commerce itself had brought in, giving the instrument of cleansing a grim irony.

All the witnesses note that the making of the scourge carries its own significance. Jesus does not act impulsively or in sudden uncontrolled anger, but pauses to fashion an instrument — a brief, deliberate act that signals purposive, sovereign action. This momentary pause — the calm plaiting of cords before the expulsion — serves as evidence, across the commentators here, of the controlled dignity with which the Son acted in his Father's house: not a loss of composure but the measured exercise of rightful authority.
16:
On John 2:16 — "Take these things hence; make not my Father's house a house of merchandise" — the witnesses converge on the claim that this language is a deliberate assertion of divine sonship, and they attend carefully to what the contrast between John's account and the Synoptic parallel reveals. Robertson analyses the Greek: the command "Take these things hence" (ἄρατε ταῦτα ἐντεῦθεν) is a first-aorist imperative addressed specifically to the dove-sellers, who required a verbal command rather than physical coercion because releasing caged birds would have meant losing them irrecoverably. The second injunction — "Stop making" (μὴ ποιεῖτε, present imperative) — implies an ongoing action that must cease permanently rather than a prohibition against something not yet begun. The two commands together cover the full scope of the desecration: removal of goods already present, and cessation of the practice going forward.

Calvin attends to the contrast between this early cleansing and the one the Synoptics record at the close of Jesus's ministry. Here in John, the language is measured: "make not my Father's house a house of merchandise." In the Synoptic account, delivered after years of continued desecration, the language escalates sharply: "You have made it a den of thieves" (Matthew 21:13). Calvin reads this progression as pedagogically deliberate — at the first cleansing a moderate reproach was appropriate; at the second, when the traders had returned undeterred, sharper public condemnation was warranted. This graduated severity reveals both the moral seriousness of the offence and the patience with which the Son acted before pronouncing final judgment on the institution.

Ellicott draws attention to Cyril of Jerusalem's contribution, which uses the "My Father's house" claim to address the Marcionite separation of the two Testaments. For Cyril, Jesus's words here — echoing his claim as a twelve-year-old in Luke 2:49 — affirm that the God worshipped in the Jerusalem temple is identical with the Father whom Christ reveals. The "Father's house" language thus does double theological work: correcting the commercial desecration of the present moment, and asserting the unbroken continuity of the divine plan from Moses through the Prophets to the Son who now stands within the very house his Father had built for Israel's worship.
17:
On John 2:17 — "The zeal of thine house hath eaten me up" (from Psalm 69:9) — the witnesses are notable for two things: the precision with which the grammatical-historical commentators analyse the tense, and the broader question of when the disciples recalled this text. Ellicott makes the sharpest textual observation: the verse should read "is written … shall eat me up" — not "hath eaten me up." The future tense of the verb in the psalm citation transforms the disciples' memory into a prophetic anticipation: they recalled a scripture that pointed forward to the consuming zeal that would cost Jesus his life, not one that merely described his temple action after the fact. Ellicott reads this as telling evidence of the disciples' actual mental state — a detail that "could be known only to one of their number," i.e., the Evangelist himself as an eyewitness.

Calvin engages the timing of the disciples' recollection. He dismisses the surface reading that the passage came to their minds immediately in the moment of the cleansing: rather, he argues, they "afterwards, when, having been taught by God, they considered with themselves what was the meaning of this passage, applied it to Christ." This is consistent with John's explicit note in v.22 that after the resurrection they "remembered" and "believed." The quotation here is thus a retrospective theological interpretation placed at the structural point of the narrative, not an instantaneous recognition. Robertson confirms the grammar: "were reminded" (ἐμνήσθησαν, first aorist passive of μιμνήσκω), a passive that implies a divine prompting of memory.

Barnes and Adam Clarke stay closer to the devotional register: the verse expresses Christ's "intense ardour" and "extraordinary concern" for the purity of his Father's house, a zeal so consuming it would eventually burn him up — the cross as the terminus of the same devoted commitment that drove the money-changers from the temple. Gill traces the Psalm to its messianic context and its citation elsewhere in the New Testament, establishing that Psalm 69 belongs canonically to the suffering Servant. Wesley's contribution here is simply the cross-reference itself (Ps 69:9), but even in its brevity it signals the typological weight that all the witnesses attach to this remembered verse.
18:
The witnesses on John 2:18 — the Jewish authorities' demand for a sign validating Jesus's authority to cleanse the temple — are both numerous and divided on the underlying logic of that demand. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the institutional and cultural context. Lightfoot notes that four centuries had elapsed without a prophet recognized by the nation: since the Spirit had departed and prophecy had ceased, any claim to divine authority had to be authenticated by wonders, and so the demand for a sign was, in its own terms, not unreasonable. Robertson adds that the traders had purchased their concession from the Sanhedrin's Sadducean and Pharisaic leadership; the demand for a sign was thus, at least partly, a challenge from those whose commercial interests had just been disrupted.

The eastern-Antiochene school, via Theophylact, raises the irony the Reformers will later amplify: the act of purging the temple with such authority ought itself to have counted as an implicit sign — was a sign really needed, Chrysostom pointedly asks (as cited through Theophylact), for the stopping of evil practices? Calvin develops this logic most fully: that no one in so large an assembly "laid hands on Christ, and none of the dealers in cattle or of the money-changers repelled him by violence" was itself a "sufficiently evident miracle" to any who were not utterly blinded — "for by the power of God alone" could one man have accomplished this undisturbed. The demand for a sign in the face of what they had just witnessed reveals, for both Calvin and the Theophylact-Chrysostom tradition, a willed blindness rather than genuine epistemic need.

Origen opens a different and more allegorical register. He reads "the Jews" who demand the sign as figures for those who remain "of the body" and incline to material things, unable to perceive the spiritual temple. Barnes and Clarke read the demand more straightforwardly against the Mosaic precedent: when Moses came to deliver Israel, he authenticated his commission with signs; the authorities were applying the same criterion to Jesus, and from within the framework of their tradition the test was not illegitimate, only inadequate. Wesley closes with a pointed remark: "they require a miracle, to confirm a miracle!" — the expulsion itself having already been a display of supernatural authority.
19:
On John 2:19 — "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up" — the witnesses are agreed that the saying is deliberately obscure and prophetically encoded, but they disagree about the precise communicative strategy involved. The Reformed tradition, through Calvin, reads the obscurity as a principled withholding: Christ "reckoned them unworthy of a direct reply," choosing to give the sign of his resurrection in a veiled form just as he elsewhere spoke in parables to those "unable to comprehend the mysteries of the heavenly kingdom" (citing Matthew 13:13). The sign is given, but given in a form that will only be unlocked by the resurrection itself.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the linguistic and legal details. Robertson notes that the imperative λύσατε ("destroy") is permissive rather than commanding: "it is the permissive imperative, not a command to do it." He also insists on the distinction between ναός (the sanctuary, the inner dwelling-place of God) and ἱερόν (the larger temple precinct), arguing that Jesus's choice of ναός points precisely to the meaning John will shortly supply — the bodily temple in which the fullness of God dwells (compare 1 Corinthians 3:16). Barnes suggests Jesus may have pointed with his finger to his own body as he spoke, and reads the imperative as carrying the force of a future: "you will destroy, and I will raise." Lightfoot supplies the legal dimension: this saying, seized upon and distorted at the trial, follows a pattern of giving real signs that are not "mere signs" — not arbitrary symbols but weighty enigmas that carry their own proof when fulfilled.

The eastern-Alexandrian voice, through Origen, interprets the saying at the level it is most comfortable with — as a statement about passion and resurrection that transcends its immediate occasion. The material temple's destruction and rebuilding is a figure for the death and resurrection of the Son, and the Jews who are "of the body" cannot perceive what the spiritually minded will later understand. Wesley's characteristic brevity here is illuminating: "This temple — Doubtless pointing, while he spoke, to his body, the temple and habitation of the Godhead" — reading gesture and word together as a single act of embodied proclamation.
20:
On John 2:20 — "Forty and six years was this temple in building, and wilt thou raise it up in three days?" — the witnesses concentrate on the historical reckoning behind the forty-six years. Lightfoot provides the most sustained argument: he insists the number refers not to the temple of Zerubbabel but to Herod's renovation, for two reasons — first, the conversation takes place in Herod's temple; second, any computation based on Zerubbabel's construction falls into chronological difficulty. He proceeds to work out the year-count from Herod's commencement, cross-checked against Josephus, concluding that the figure is coherent and historically grounded.

Barnes and Clarke independently note that Herod began the major rebuilding work in the eighteenth year of his reign. Josephus (Antiquities xv.11.1 and xx.9.5–7) is the primary source both cite: Herod finished the main edifice in nine and a half years but continued subsidiary construction throughout his reign and beyond, so that the building was not completed until A.D. 64, just six or seven years before its destruction. The forty-six years of the Jewish interlocutors' claim is therefore not an exaggeration but a statement about ongoing construction still visible to any visitor in Jerusalem. Ellicott adds that the same Josephan source gives the start date as the Nisan of 734 A.U.C. (approximately 20–19 B.C.), and Robertson confirms the chronological arithmetic.

Origen raises a different kind of difficulty: he cannot reconcile the forty-six years with the Books of Kings, which give a three-year preparation period and a seven-year construction period for Solomon's temple. His solution is to conclude that the Jews' reference must be to Herod's rebuilding — the same conclusion Lightfoot reaches by historical reasoning rather than textual inference. Wesley adds the memorable detail that additional repairs continued "thirty-six years longer, till within six or seven years of the destruction of the state, city, and temple" — grounding the verse in the full arc of Herodian building, from its beginning in the generation before Jesus to its completion just before the catastrophe of 70 A.D. Calvin finds in the forty-six-year figure a coincidence with Daniel's weeks (Daniel 9:25), an intriguing typological connection he pursues briefly before moving on.
21:
On John 2:21 — "But he spake of the temple of his body" — the witnesses are in broad agreement on the interpretive principle but differ on how fully to press the typological identification between Christ's body and the temple. The grammatical-historical commentators are most restrained. Robertson notes that this is the Evangelist's own retrospective explanation — "John's view as he looks back at it, not what he understood when Jesus spoke the words" — and emphasises the imperfect tense (ἔλεγεν, "he had been speaking"), marking the narrator's after-the-fact interpretive stance rather than the disciples' contemporary comprehension. Ellicott similarly observes that this is the disciples' solution to the enigma, read "in the after history," and points to the striking parallel in Mark 14:58, where a spiritual temple is also contrasted with the material one.

John Gill presses the typological identification most systematically among the Protestant commentators. He assembles a catena of New Testament texts: the bodies of believers are called temples (1 Corinthians 6:19), the human nature of Christ is called a tabernacle, and Christ in prophecy is designated "a sanctuary" (citing Isaiah 8:14). The fulness of the Godhead dwelling in Christ's human nature is what makes his body a temple in the truest and most concentrated sense. Adam Clarke specifies the logic: Christ's body is "the temple, his body — the temple of his Divinity — the place in which, as in the ancient temple, his Godhead dwelt." He also notes the perversion of these words by the false witnesses at the trial (citing Matthew 26:60), showing how the enigmatic form of the saying enabled its misuse.

Origen, true to the eastern-Alexandrian mode, asks whether the identification of body and temple is to be taken in its plain sense or pressed further, and suggests the saying reaches even deeper into the unity of the divine and human in Christ's person. Lightfoot supplies what is perhaps the most evocative contextual note: the second temple — Herod's magnificent edifice — lacked the fire from heaven, the ark with the mercy seat, the Urim and Thummim, the Shekinah glory, and the Holy Spirit in prophetic operation. Against this catalogue of absences, Christ's body is proposed as the true and complete temple, in which all these divine presences are gathered and exceeded.
22:
The witnesses on John 2:22 — "When therefore he was risen from the dead, his disciples remembered that he had said this unto them; and they believed the scripture and the word which Jesus had said" — converge on the hermeneutical insight that resurrection is the key that unlocks the meaning of a saying that had been opaque during Jesus's ministry. Barnes states the general principle most clearly: "Many prophecies are obscure when spoken which are perfectly plain when the event takes place." The disciples treasured the saying in memory without understanding it, and the event — the resurrection — disclosed what the words had always meant. Origen develops this further: the disciples "saw that what He had said about the temple had a higher application to His passion and His resurrection," which means that for John and his community the scripture and the prophetic word of Jesus are read together through the lens of the resurrection event.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply important textual notes. Clarke registers that the words "unto them" (αὐτοῖς) are absent from many significant manuscripts and versions (the Syriac, Persian, Arabic, Coptic, Ethiopic, Armenian, Slavonic, Vulgate, and Old Latin, as well as Griesbach's critical text), suggesting that "they remembered that he had said this" refers to the disciples' general memory of the saying rather than a specific private teaching. Robertson confirms that the disciples' recollection came "not at first then, but only slowly after the disciples themselves were convinced" of the resurrection. Ellicott traces the career of the saying through its misuse: it fixed itself in men's memories as something powerful and strange, surfaces as the accusation of false witnesses at the trial, and is thrown back at Jesus on the cross — in this way "the death on the cross" becomes, through the very accusation levelled against it, "the means of its accomplishment."

The theological weight of the verse, as Calvin reads it, confirms his earlier argument that the disciples' citation of Psalm 69 in v.17 was retrospective: it is only after the resurrection that the disciples "believed the scripture and the word which Jesus had said," meaning that both the Old Testament text and the enigmatic temple saying become luminous simultaneously, each interpreting the other in the light of the empty tomb. Wesley's final note here is pointed: "they believed the scripture, and the word which Jesus had said — Concerning his resurrection" — the resurrection is the hermeneutical event that binds testimony together.
23:
On John 2:23 the witnesses converge on a crucial distinction: many "believed" in Jesus during the Passover feast on account of his miracles, yet the next two verses will show that Jesus did not "believe in" or entrust himself to them. The question is what kind of belief is in view. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators all press the distinction. Clarke is blunt: they "believed him to be the promised Messiah, but did not believe in him to the salvation of their souls." Calvin reads the cold faith of the Jerusalem crowds as a "shadow of faith" — it existed, but not in a form worthy of the name, and the fact that Jesus "produced no good effect on them by many miracles — except that they entertained a cold faith" proves retroactively that they did not deserve further disclosure. John Wesley draws the line more economically: "Many believed — That he was a teacher sent from God" — no more, no less.

The patristic voices develop this by attending to what the miracles actually accomplished. Bede opens by framing the structure of the chapter: what Jesus did on the way to Jerusalem, and how others responded to him there. John Chrysostom (whose comment appears to be primarily about perseverance and surface versus deep belief) and Origen both read the crowd's belief as belonging to the category of shallow seed — present on the surface, lacking root. Origen presses the anthropological question the verse poses: how is it that Jesus "did not believe" those who believed in him? His answer moves toward the Johannine theme of divine omniscience: Jesus perceived the interior state of his respondents and found there no genuine transformation.

Matthew Henry and Augustine, both operating in the broader scope of the Passover section, observe that Jesus took the Passover season — with its unparalleled concentration of pilgrims — as an opportunity to preach and perform miracles. The crowd's response was quantitatively impressive ("many believed") but qualitatively deficient. A.T. Robertson supplies the lexical foundation: the Greek ἐπίστευσαν ("believed") here and the ἐπίστευεν ("entrusted") of v.24 are the same verb used in two different senses, the double usage revealing what authentic faith demands and what this admiring but superficial crowd lacked.
24:
On John 2:24 — "But Jesus did not commit himself unto them, because he knew all" — the witnesses concentrate on two related questions: what Jesus's non-commitment (οὐκ ἐπίστευεν ἑαυτὸν) means practically, and what the claim to know "all" or "all men" reveals about his person. The grammatical-historical school is most precise on the practical dimension. Robertson supplies the illuminating parallel from Acts 8:13, where Simon Magus "believed" and was baptised but was genuinely unsaved — a case of externally valid but spiritually hollow faith. The negative imperfect ("kept on refusing to trust himself to them") indicates a settled, ongoing disposition rather than a one-time decision. Barnes unpacks the idiom: "Jesus did not put trust or reliance in them … He acted cautiously and prudently. The proper time for him to die had not come, and he secured his own safety."

The Reformed commentators read the verse at a deeper level. Calvin, diverging from Augustine's reading about recent converts and from the more cautious reading of simply "being on guard," argues that the Evangelist's meaning is that Christ "did not choose to be a disciple or scholar of theirs" — he did not accept their offer of human approval as the standard by which his ministry should be governed. This is not mere prudence but a theological refusal: Christ does not need human validation. Calvin's reading of the verse is thus not about self-protection but about the independence of Christ's mission from popular endorsement — his authority derived from the Father, not from the believing crowds. John Gill adds the variant that some ancient commentators understood this as Christ not committing "the whole of the Gospel" to them — reserving fuller disclosure for his chosen apostles.

Wesley draws a pointed pastoral application: "Let us learn hence not rashly to put ourselves into the power of others. Let us study a wise and happy medium between universal suspiciousness and that easiness which would make us the property of every pretender to kindness and respect." Ellicott, while endorsing the basic reading, notices what the verse reveals about Christ's gaze: the eye that perceived Nathanael's interior in John 1:47 now looks through the Jerusalem crowd's surface enthusiasm to "the very depth of their hearts," finding there an unbroken ledge of unbelief beneath the shallow water of their admiration.
25:
On John 2:25 — "for he knew what was in man" — the witnesses close the chapter with a brief but theologically weighty verse that gathers the preceding observations about Jesus's omniscience into a general principle. Barnes states the doctrinal inference most directly: to know what is in man is "the prerogative of God alone" (Jeremiah 17:10). Since Jesus possessed this knowledge inherently and universally — "because he had made all" (John 1:3) and "because he was God" (John 1:1) — the verse functions as a final christological assertion embedded in a narrative about human shallowness. The crowd's inability to give Jesus what he did not need (testimony about themselves) is the photographic negative of Christ's perfect knowledge of what they actually were.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the syntax. Robertson notes the imperfect tense (χρείαν εἶχεν, "he did not have need") — not merely that he lacked the need on one occasion but that he habitually, continuously, did not require external testimony about any human person. Ellicott distinguishes between v.24 and v.25: the former is the particular instance (he did not commit himself to this crowd), and the latter is the universal principle (he never needed anyone to testify about any human being, because he read human nature directly). Clarke highlights the variant reading: where most manuscripts have "all men" (πάντας), some important witnesses read "all things" (πάντα) — a variant that Clarke prefers, on the ground that knowing all things is the corollary of having made all things and being the all-wise God of John 1:1.

Calvin, drawing together the chapter's closing movement, argues that the Evangelist's purpose in vv.23–25 is to explain why Jesus did not reveal himself more openly to the Jerusalem crowds: he discerned by his own divine intuition exactly what quality of faith was there, and that discernment — rather than any external report or social pressure — governed his conduct. John Gill identifies the capacity to distinguish at once between the wicked and the good as one of the marks by which the Messiah was expected to be recognized, citing the prophetic tradition. Wesley closes with characteristic economy: Jesus knew what was in man — "Namely, a desperately deceitful heart" — an allusion to Jeremiah 17:9 that wraps the whole chapter's meditation on inadequate faith in the prophetic diagnosis of the human condition.
